metaTitle: The Boeing Atmosphere Global Documentation Hub - Components - Navigation rail

header.title: Navigation rail
header.description: Navigation rails enable users to navigate between the first level of an app's navigation on mid-sized and large-sized devices

items: Use navigation rails in medium, large, and extra-large screen sizes
Ideally, navigation rails should contain 3-7 destinations, plus an optional FAB, but they can accommodate up to 10 destinations
Navigation rails should always be placed at the same place, even on different screens of an app
Keep destination labels short and concise to avoid text truncation
Always use the provided Atmosphere Design System Icons Library

data.resource: Figma: Atmosphere Foundations and Components Library

sections.textBefore:
Types

The Navigation rail can be used in the following three configurations. Refer to the Usage section in Guidelines for their appropriate use cases.

States

The destinations within the navigation rail come in eight different states to effectively communicate the status of the component. Refer to the selection behavior guidelines for their appropriate use cases.

Configurations

Below are some commonly used configurations of the navigation rail component. However, the navigation rail component can accommodate other configurations to meet the app's requirements.
sections.textAfter:
1. Without logo
  2. Without FAB
  3. All destinations with text labels
  4. Without profile
  5. Without logo, profile, and icon button
  6. Without label

sections.textBefore:
Usage

The navigation rail is a side navigation component that can display three to ten destinations, and can optionally contain a FAB button, a logo, an icon button, and a user profile.
sections.textAfter:
Below are some of its usage characteristics:

  • Each destination is represented by an icon and label text. The label text appears as a tooltip when hovering over the destination in the navigation rail, without a label.
  • One navigation destination is always active.
  • The navigation rail is recommended for medium, large, and extra-large viewports; it switches to a navigation bar on smaller devices.
  • Navigation rails should only be used for top-level destinations that need to be accessible anywhere in an app.

Navigation rails should not be used for:

  • Small and extra-small viewports
  • Secondary navigation destinations
  • Destinations with longer label text

1. Container
  2. Logomark (optional)
  3. FAB (optional)
  4. Icon - active
  5. Active indicator
  6. Label text - active (optional)
  7. Icon - inactive
  8. Label text - inactive (optional)
  9. Small badge
  10. Overflow menu
  11. Icon button (optional)
  12. Large badge
  13. User profile (optional)

sections.text:
Logomark

By default, the Boeing logo mark should always remain visible inside the navigation rail.
The logo mark could be hidden when the navigation rail is used in combination with the top app bar; in such cases, the logo should be visible in the top app bar.

Icon

Navigation rail destinations should always be represented by icons that symbolize the content or the nature of a destination.

Active indicator

The active indicator is a background shape communicating which destination of the navigation rail is currently being displayed.

Label text

Label texts are short, meaningful descriptions of navigation destinations.

Labels should be kept short enough to avoid truncation.

If label texts cannot be further shortened, consider using a wide navigation rail or navigation drawer instead.

Label text appears as a tooltip on hover action in the navigation rail without label.

Overflow menu

The overflow menu appears when the listed navigation destinations do not fit within the device viewport height.

Place the more important destinations towards the top of the navigation rail, and the least used destinations towards the bottom.

Badge

Navigation rail icons can include badges on the upper right corner of the icon.

  1. Small badge
  2. Large (single-digit)
  3. Large (double-digit)
  4. Large (more than double-digit)

Icon button

The navigation rail features three icon buttons: notifications, settings, and help. By default, the notification icon button is enabled at the bottom section of the navigation rail.

  • The notification icon button can be swapped with either of the remaining options.
  • The navigation rail should always display the most frequently used icon button, while the other two options can be configured within the user profile section.

User profile

The navigation rail also provides access to the user profile drop-down menu, represented by a thumbnail image of the user or the user's name initial, placed at the bottom of the navigation rail.

sections.textBefore:
Placement

The navigation rail should always run vertically along the side of a layout.

The navigation rail could be paired with a modal navigation drawer to display additional secondary destinations or actions.

Responsive layout

When transitioning from medium and large viewports to small viewports, the navigation rail transforms into a modal navigation drawer.

sections.text:
Scrolling

Destinations in the navigation rail should remain visible when scrolling vertically.

On smaller viewport heights, more than three destinations should get grouped inside overflow menu.

In the case of horizontal scroll layouts, the navigation rail should remain fixed and the content should scroll underneath the rail.

Selection

When tapping a destination within the navigation rail, the destination screen should use a top-level transition pattern.

The navigation rail remains in active state while interacting within the rail, while interacting anywhere outside the navigation rail puts the rail into an inactive state.

Use cases

Users should be able to:

  • Navigate between navigation destinations using assistive technology.
  • Select a particular navigation destination from a set or from an overflow menu dropdown.
  • Read the destination label through a tooltip upon hover and focus state on the navigation rail without label option.
  • Access the navigation rail with label option when switching from a large viewport to a medium viewport.

Cursor

Navigation rail (with label): When hovering over a destination, the hover state appears, providing a visual cue that the destination is interactive.

Navigation rail (without label): When hovering over a destination, a label tooltip appears along with the hover state, providing a visual cue of an interactive element.

Upon clicking, the icon of the selected destination switches from outlined to filled, along with a background color change.

Keyboard

Users should be able to navigate to and select an active item using the specified keys.

data.actions: Focus lands on the first navigation destination

sections.text:
Initial focus

Initial focus lands directly on the first navigation item, as its the first interactive element of the component.

  • First tab on first item
  • Second tab on second item
  • Space/enter on same item
  • First tab on first item (as Navigation rail doesn’t have a logo and a FAB button)

Labeling elements

The accessibility label for a navigation item is typically the same as the adjacent text label.

If the UI text is correctly linked to the switch, assistive tech (such as a screenreader) will read the UI text followed by the component’s role.
